annotator S.report_text:
The attempts involved a phishing email appearing to be from the U.S. Department of State with links to zip files containing malicious Windows shortcuts that delivered Cobalt Strike Beacon.
The attacker appears to have compromised the email server of a hospital and the corporate website of a consulting company in order to use their infrastructure to send phishing emails.
The attacker used unique links in each phishing email.
The threat actor crafted the phishing emails to masquerade as a U.S. Department of State Public Affairs official sharing an official document.
The links led to a ZIP archive that contained a weaponized Windows shortcut file hosted on a likely compromised legitimate domain.
The shortcut file was crafted to execute a PowerShell command that read, decoded, and executed additional code from within the shortcut file.
The BEACON payload was configured with a modified variation of the publicly available "Pandora" Malleable C2 Profile and used a command and control (C2) domain.
Each phishing email contained a unique malicious URL, likely for tracking victim clicks.
ds7002.lnk was a malicious shortcut (LNK) file that contained an embedded BEACON DLL and decoy PDF, and was crafted to launch a PowerShell command.
the PowerShell command extracted and executed the Cobalt Strike BEACON backdoor and decoy PDF.
This command included some specific obfuscation, which may indicate attempts to bypass specific detection logic. For example, the use of 'FromBase'+0x40+'String', in place of FromBase64String, the PowerShell command used to decode base64.
The PowerShell loader code is obfuscated.
The dropped BEACON loader DLL was executed by RunDll32.exe using the export function "PointFunctionCall":
